_meta.purpose: Sub-state income taxes: city, county, school district and special-district levies that sit on top of state income tax. Researched July 2026 against jurisdiction and state revenue sources.
_meta.whyItMatters: Our audience is mostly self-employed, and the local rule for SELF-EMPLOYMENT NET PROFIT is frequently different from the wage rule. Every entry records appliesToSelfEmploymentNetProfit separately for that reason, and null there means 'not confirmed' — never 'no'.
shapes: percentage of state taxable income (Maryland counties, Indiana counties)
percentage of gross wages or net profit (Kentucky, Michigan, Missouri, Delaware, Alabama)
percentage OF THE STATE TAX rather than of income (Yonkers resident surcharge)
flat dollars per week worked, not a rate at all (West Virginia city service fees)
flat dollars per year (Portland Arts Tax)
threshold levies on income above a floor (Oregon Metro SHS, Multnomah PFA)
_meta.status: Ohio and Pennsylvania — the two most complex local-tax states — are still being researched and are absent from this file.
_meta.crossChecked: Indiana, Kentucky and Alabama were researched twice independently. The second pass RESOLVED Alabama's open question — the occupational tax does not reach self-employment profit — and resolved Kentucky's contested school levy. It also surfaced that Indiana's 2026 STATE rate is 2.95%, not the 3.00% in the legacy dataset, which is now corrected in state-brackets-2026.json. Michigan, Missouri, Delaware, West Virginia and Oregon were also researched twice. The second pass confirmed Saginaw's rate against the city's own form and identified the widely-used Grand Rapids aggregate table as stale; corrected Delaware's head tax from the commonly quoted $10 to $15; found Weirton's 2026 increase from $2 to $5 and Fairmont's inverted resident rule; and established that Oregon's statewide transit tax stayed at 0.1% because Measure 120 failed in May 2026, contradicting many payroll guides.
_meta.fetchBlocked: michigan.gov, kcmo.gov and wilmingtonde.gov all refuse automated fetching. Figures for those came from alternative official sources — Michigan Treasury form PDFs, the St. Louis Comptroller's comparison page, and Wilmington's own budget portal.

nycResidentTax.appliesTo: NYC residents and part-year residents only
nycResidentTax.source: tax.ny.gov IT-201-I NYC rate schedule; NYSDTF confirmed no NYC change for 2026

nycUnincorporatedBusinessTax.note: A separate 4% tax on unincorporated businesses with NYC-source income — sole proprietors and partnerships. Unlike the personal income tax it DOES reach nonresidents. Exemptions and the resident credit were not researched; do not model without further work.
nycUnincorporatedBusinessTax.source: nyc.gov Department of Finance UBT page

yonkersResidentSurcharge.note: 16.75% OF your New York STATE income tax, not of income. A rate on a tax, which no other jurisdiction in this file does.

NY._notes: NYC has no nonresident personal income tax — the commuter tax was repealed in 1999. But the 4% UBT does hit nonresident sole proprietors with NYC-source income, which is the trap for our audience.

MD.shape: percentage of Maryland taxable income, set by COUNTY OF RESIDENCE not county of work
MD.source: marylandcomptroller.gov 2026 state and local withholding memo, Attachment 1
MD._notes: Mandatory for every Maryland resident, levied on the same base as the state tax so it automatically reaches self-employment profit, capital gains and interest. Allegany rose 3.03->3.20 and Kent 3.20->3.30 for 2026.

IN.shape: percentage of Indiana adjusted gross income — same base as the state tax
IN.nonresidentRule: County is fixed as of 1 January. An out-of-state resident on 1 January whose principal place of work or business is in an Indiana county pays that county's FULL rate — there is no longer a reduced nonresident rate.
IN._notes: All 92 counties levy it, on the same base as the state tax, so it reaches self-employment profit in full. Porter is the cheapest at 0.5% and Randolph the dearest at 3.0% — a six-fold spread between neighbouring counties. Six counties changed rates effective 1 January 2026: Carroll, Grant, Greene, Howard, Shelby, Union. The odd non-round values (Brown 0.025234, Carroll 0.024733, Jasper 0.02864, Whitley 0.016829) are exact as published and must not be rounded. Re-pull dn01.pdf each January. Confirmed twice, independently, from the same DOR notice.

KY.shape: occupational licence fee on gross wages AND net profits, levied by cities, counties and some school districts

Louisville / Jefferson County.note: The 0.75% school-board component does not apply to nonresident individuals, hence the lower nonresident rate. Confirmed from TY2024/25 official form instructions; louisvilleky.gov blocks automated fetch so no 2026-specific confirmation.

Fayette County Public Schools.note: RESOLVED by a second independent pass: the increase to 0.75% was declared unlawful and VOID by Kentucky Attorney General opinion OAG 25-07, for failure to give the required week of public notice. The rate in force remains 0.5%, stacking on LFUCG's 2.25% for a combined 2.75%. Confidence medium — FCPS's own page carries no year stamp, and SB 76 was filed in January 2026 to restrict such levies further.

KY._notes: These reach net profits of sole proprietors and contractors explicitly, with no personal exemptions or deductions. Both a city and a county may tax the same income, though counties over 30,000 population must credit city tax paid.
KY.stackingRule: KRS 68.197 requires a credit against the COUNTY tax for amounts paid to a city within that county, where the county has 30,000+ population. Stacking a city rate and a county rate without applying the credit overstates liability — this is the single easiest way to get Kentucky wrong.

AL.shape: municipal occupational tax on gross wages for work performed in the city
AL._notes: RESOLVED by a second independent pass, and the answer is no. Alabama's occupational tax is a WAGE tax: Birmingham's ordinance keys it to compensation where 'the relationship between the individual performing the services and the person for whom such services are rendered is the legal relationship of employer and employee'. Self-employed people are instead reached by the separate municipal BUSINESS LICENSE tax on gross receipts, which is a different levy with different rates by classification. Do NOT apply Alabama occupational tax to Schedule C profit. Auburn and Opelika confirmed from their own city sites; the remaining ~22 cities come from an undated Alabama League of Municipalities survey and are low confidence. Act 2020-14 freezes NEW occupational taxes without legislative approval, so the roster is stable, but existing cities can still change their rate.

MI.shape: city income tax under the Uniform City Income Tax Ordinance
MI.generalRule: Nonresident rate is one-half the resident rate by statute, and nonresidents are taxed only on income earned inside the city. Residents pay on all income wherever earned.
MI._notes: 24 cities levy a city income tax. Statutory default is 1% resident / 0.5% nonresident (MCL 141.611), with four exceptions: Detroit 2.4/1.2, Highland Park 2.0/1.0, Grand Rapids 1.5/0.75, Saginaw 1.5/0.75. The base is compensation AND net profits of an unincorporated business (MCL 141.612(b)), so self-employment profit is taxed — residents on all of it, nonresidents on the city-earned portion. Pensions, annuities and Social Security are NOT subject. DATA-QUALITY WARNING, confirmed twice: the convenient all-cities table on grandrapidsmi.gov is STALE — it lists Saginaw at 1.0/0.5, contradicted by Saginaw's own S-1040 instructions at 1.5/0.75. Do not use that table as a primary source. Only 8 of the 24 cities were verified from their own forms; the other 16 rest on the statutory default plus that same table.

statutoryDefault.authority: MCL 141.611; the nonresident rate may never exceed half the resident rate

MO.shape: 1% earnings tax on wages and on net profits of businesses and self-employed persons
MO._notes: Both were renewed by voters on 7 April 2026 for a further five years, so both are confirmed in force. Residents pay on all earnings wherever performed; nonresidents pay on work actually performed inside the city and can claim a refund for out-of-city days. Both explicitly reach net profits, so a sole proprietor pays 1% on city-attributable profit. St. Louis's 0.5% payroll expense tax is employer-side and is not part of an individual's calculation.

DE.shape: flat percentage on gross wages, plus a parallel net profits tax at the same rate

Wilmington.headTaxNote: $15 per employee per month on businesses with 6+ employees, assessed on headcount minus 5. The widely quoted $10 is STALE. It is an EMPLOYER tax and must never be deducted from a paycheck calculation.

DE._notes: Wilmington is Delaware's only municipal wage tax, confirmed from the City's own FY2027 budget tax-rate sheet and unchanged across FY2025-FY2027. 1.25% on gross earned income, paid at the SAME rate by residents and by nonresidents working in the city — unlike Michigan there is no half-rate for nonresidents. A separate Net Profits Tax, also 1.25%, covers sole proprietors and partnerships. The rate is set by the Delaware General Assembly, not the city.

WV.shape: flat dollars per week worked — NOT a percentage, and must never be modelled as one
WV.appliesToSelfEmploymentNetProfit: n/a as a rate; self-employed people working in the city are liable for the flat weekly fee, confirmed for Huntington

Weirton.note: RAISED FROM $2.00 IN 2026 — Ordinance 2272 passed second reading 14 April 2026, effective about mid-May. Confirmed by news reporting only; the official ordinance PDF is an unreadable scan. Most aggregators still say $2.00.

Charleston.note: $3.00 since January 2018. Some sources quote '$6 per pay period' — that is the biweekly equivalent, not a different rate.

Morgantown.note: An increase was under discussion through May 2026 but had not been adopted. Recheck before shipping.

Wheeling.note: Source document dated 2019, effective January 2020. No later change found, but no 2026-dated confirmation either.

Fairmont.note: INVERTS THE USUAL PATTERN. Only NONRESIDENT employees pay the weekly payroll charge; Fairmont residents are exempt from it and instead pay $8.67 a month on their city services bill.

WV._notes: A different shape from every other entry: a fixed dollar amount per week worked, regardless of hours or earnings. Anyone working at a physical location in the city pays, residents and commuters alike. Annual cost is roughly the amount times weeks worked. Not an exhaustive list of WV cities.
WV.namingTrap: Match on the fee's BASE, not its name. Huntington has two: the CITY Service Fee is the $5/week payroll fee, while its MUNICIPAL Service Fee is a property-owner fire fee and is not a wage item at all. Other cities use 'Municipal Service Fee' to mean the payroll fee.
WV.authority: W. Va. Code 8-13-13, special charges for municipal services

OR.shape: threshold levies on Oregon taxable income above a floor, plus one flat annual charge

Metro Supportive Housing Services.note: Thresholds rose from $125,000/$200,000 because Metro Council adopted CPI indexing effective 1 January 2026; the rate did not change. Metro publishes updated thresholds by 15 November each year.

Multnomah County Preschool for All.note: The scheduled +0.8 percentage-point increase DOES NOT APPLY IN 2026 — it was delayed by Board ordinance to 1 January 2027. For 2026 the rates remain 1.5% and 3.0%. Thresholds are NOT indexed, unlike SHS.

Portland Arts Tax.note: A flat annual charge, not a rate, and not prorated for part-year residents. Payable by Portland residents 18 or over with $1,000 or more of income, above the federal poverty level.

OR._notes: Both Metro SHS and Multnomah PFA use Oregon taxable income, so a sole proprietor's Schedule C profit counts toward the thresholds and is taxed. Both reach NONRESIDENTS on district-sourced income — the well-known trap for Washington-state commuters who owe Metro SHS on Portland-source income despite living in a no-income-tax state. The statewide transit tax and TriMet/Lane payroll taxes are employer-side or statewide and are excluded.
OR.remoteWorkRule: A nonresident who physically travels into the district to work IS taxable on that work. A nonresident employed by a district employer who works ENTIRELY REMOTELY from outside it — the Vancouver, Washington home office — is NOT. This is the distinction that decides the answer for most cross-river commuters and should be modelled explicitly rather than assumed.

statewideTransitTax.note: STAYED AT 0.1% FOR 2026. The 2025 transportation package would have doubled it to 0.2% from 1 January 2026, but Measure 120 was DEFEATED at the 19 May 2026 primary, so DOR instructs employers to continue withholding 0.1%. Many payroll-vendor 2026 guides still say 0.2% and are wrong.

employerTaxesNotPersonal.note: Employer-side, not part of an individual's calculation — but both have parallel SELF-EMPLOYMENT versions charged on net earnings over $400 from services in the district, which are.
